news_summary: Today's health news highlights advancements in gene editing for disease treatment, breakthroughs in biochemical processes, new insights into appetite regulation, and fascinating discoveries about shared laughter in humans and great apes.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité de la santé d'aujourd'hui met en avant les progrès réalisés dans le domaine de l'édition génétique pour le traitement des maladies, les avancées dans les processus biochimiques, les nouvelles perspectives sur la régulation de l'appétit, ainsi que des découvertes fascinantes concernant le rire partagé entre les humains et les grands singes.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de salud de hoy destacan los avances en la edición genética para el tratamiento de enfermedades, los avances en los procesos bioquímicos, los nuevos conocimientos sobre la regulación del apetito y los fascinantes descubrimientos sobre la risa compartida entre los seres humanos y los grandes simios.

articles.title: CRISPR’s next act: the companies editing the epigenome to treat disease
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01976-wA handful of start-up firms are testing therapies that target specific epigenetic markers to treat everything from high cholesterol to a rare muscular disorder.
articles.content:
In late 2021, Amber Salzman interviewed for a job that she had no intention of taking. A relatively new start-up company, called Epicrispr Biotechnologies, was looking for a chief executive, and it was keen on vetting Salzman — who had decades of experience in the pharmaceutical industry — for the role. She had said yes to the meeting only as a favour to a recruiter, who had helped her to fill a key position at another company she had worked with. Joining the start-up wasn’t something she was enthusiastic about.

Halfway through the meeting, she changed her mind. Salzman had watched as Stanley Qi, the founder of Epicrispr, drew diagrams on a whiteboard explaining that the company wanted to make a genetic therapy — not by editing the code itself, but by changing the chemical markers attached to DNA, which can switch genes on or off. Then Salzman asked another team member: “‘What disease are we going after?’ And she said, ‘FSHD’.”

How ageing changes our genes — huge epigenetic atlas gives clearest picture yet

Salzman knew the condition all too well. FSHD, short for facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy, is an inherited disorder in which muscle problems first begin in the face and upper body and can spread to other parts, sometimes requiring wheelchair use. Salzman’s husband of more than 35 years had several cousins and a grandmother with the disease, although he had not inherited it himself.

Her family’s experience of this disorder had always been on her mind, but Salzman had never seen a way to make a difference in her previous positions: “At the time, nobody really understood what caused it.” But the conversation with Epicrispr gave her a chance to address the disease.

She took up the company’s offer to become its chief executive. In doing so, Salzman joined a niche group of drug developers trying to advance a technique called targeted epigenetic editing. The idea is to remove or add epigenetic markers — essentially chemical groups that sit on DNA (and the proteins that it is wound around). Depending on which chemical group is present or absent, genes can be activated or switched off.

Some existing medications influence epigenetic markers, but these drugs act broadly and lack specificity. This new cadre of scientists has found ways to precisely alter the epigenetic markers influencing specific genes. Epicrispr, based in South San Franscisco, California, is one of several companies working on such therapies. At the International Research Congress on FSHD, held in late June in Chicago, Illinois, it became one of the first to announce data from an epigenetic-editing trial.

Epigenetic markers have a huge impact on how our cells interpret DNA. Changing the epigenetic tags on a genome is akin to using an audio mixing board to alter a piece of music to sound like the works of composer Franz Schubert or pop star Taylor Swift, says biologist Fyodor Urnov at the University of California, Berkeley. Urnov helped to pioneer the use of various gene-editing technologies and co-founded an epigenetic-editing company called Tune Therapeutics in Seattle, Washington.

Epigenetic editing: from concept to clinic

The tools being deployed in this new era of epigenetic editing put a twist on standard gene editing, which involves using the CRISPR system to cut DNA. That system is precise, but even so, it can lead to cuts in the wrong place, which can disrupt or damage genes. “Epigenetic editing is a truly exciting concept for therapeutics because there is no chance of off-target DNA mutations being made, as is the case with gene editing,” explains Jessica Tyler, a molecular biologist at Weill Cornell Medicine in New York City.

Most epigenetic-editing platforms, rather than making changes to the DNA itself, modify the markers attached to DNA. That is thought to be safer for two reasons: first, the system can’t mistakenly cut in the wrong place, and second, it reduces the possibility that the DNA could rearrange itself — which is a risk whenever DNA breaks. In addition, preclinical experiments in human cells show that epigenetic modifications are reversible.

But epigenetic forces are potent, and researchers should proceed with caution, says Yann Joly, a bioethicist who heads McGill University’s Centre of Genomics and Policy in Montreal, Canada. “Epigenetic regulation plays a central role in development and reproduction,” he says. The community needs to ensure that epigenetic therapies are delivered safely and without unintended consequences, he says.

Dead right

In 2012 and 2013, several independent groups published a series of papers describing the original CRISPR–Cas9 editing system and its application1–3. In conventional CRISPR, a guide RNA finds the target sequence in the genome, and a Cas9 enzyme then cuts the DNA. The discoveries garnered huge attention for their potential to rewrite DNA. But at the time, most people might not have appreciated that biologists were already contemplating how to adapt CRISPR editing to modulate gene expression, rather than break or rewrite the genetic code.

One such biologist was Qi, who had worked in the lab of CRISPR pioneer Jennifer Doudna at the University of California, Berkeley. He wanted to know how to control a cell’s programming, rather than altering its code.

He launched his lab at the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF), and started working out how to modify the CRISPR–Cas9 system so that it would still grab on to the targeted DNA but wouldn’t snip the sequence after reaching it. In 2013, Qi and his colleagues, including biochemist Jonathan Weissman, also at UCSF at the time, and Doudna, landed on modifications that achieved just that4. They called the repurposed Cas9 ‘dead’ because it lacked its normal enzymatic cutting activity.

World-first: therapy to make cells young again trialled in a person

Next, the team deployed a guide RNA to lead the dead Cas9 to the right place, along with a protein that could turn gene expression on and off. The tests showed that the system worked in human cells and was highly precise5. “That’s when we knew this was a transformational tool,” says Weissman, who is now based at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in Cambridge.

Not long after the publication of these key papers, Qi moved his lab to Stanford University in California. There, he continued to improve on the dead-Cas9 system and found a smaller version — called Cas12F — that could more easily be delivered to cells (the typical Cas9 protein, from bacteria, is relatively large).

Qi and his teammates found Cas12F in archaea, organisms that resemble bacteria in some ways but are evolutionarily distinct and have different cell walls. Whereas Cas9 is made up of around 1,300 amino acids, Cas12F consists of around 500. To deliver the payload to cells, the recipe for dead Cas12F is coded into a virus, known as adeno-associated virus, which is considered harmless to the body. The virus is infused into the body, and cells then produce the Cas12F construct themselves. The protein then gets to work on the target epigenetic markers.

Meanwhile, the company that Weissman co-founded, nChroma in Boston, Massachusetts, has made improvements to another component of the system: the methyltransferase element, which modifies the epigenetic markers. The firm hasn’t disclosed which one it is using but says that it is efficient and small. “I think that’s part of our secret sauce, frankly,” says Jenny Marlowe, chief development officer at nChroma.

Treatment try-outs

In 2025, a team including scientists at nChroma published a study in mice and monkeys showing that their approach worked6. The team’s epigenetic-editing system, encapsulated in lipid nanoparticles and delivered intravenously, could quash the production of a protein called PCSK9, which promotes ‘bad’ cholesterol. A single injection lowered monkeys’ levels of this type of cholesterol by around 70%.

Other epigenetic-editing therapies are moving into clinical testing. In January, nChroma began administering the first doses of an experimental epigenetic silencer against the hepatitis B virus to people with chronic infection. According to the World Health Organization, an estimated 240 million people worldwide have chronic hepatitis B — which can cause liver failure and cancer. A vaccine exists, but data from 2019 suggest that 15% of children around the world do not receive the full immunization regimen, and an increasing number of parents in countries such as the United States are refusing it for their children because of health misinformation.

To make matters worse, existing drugs cannot fully clear hepatitis B from the body because the pathogen has a nasty trick up its sleeve: bits of its genome integrate into a person’s DNA and from there, generate proteins that alter the immune response against it.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant advancement in medical science where start-up companies are developing targeted epigenetic editing therapies using CRISPR technology. These therapies aim to treat serious diseases such as facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy, high cholesterol, and chronic hepatitis B by precisely modifying epigenetic markers without altering DNA sequences, offering safer and potentially reversible treatment options. The impact is broad, addressing widespread health issues with detailed context on the technology, trials, and potential benefits, making it an inspiring breakthrough in health.
articles.personality_title: New gene-editing approach targets diseases by changing DNA markers without cutting the DNA
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have developed a new way to treat diseases by changing chemical markers on DNA instead of cutting or rewriting the DNA itself. This method is called targeted epigenetic editing and uses a tool related to CRISPR, a technology famous for gene editing.

**What happened** – A start-up company named Epicrispr Biotechnologies, led by Amber Salzman since 2021, is testing this new therapy to treat facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy (FSHD), a rare muscle disease. The company uses a modified version of CRISPR called “dead Cas9” or Cas12F, which attaches to DNA but doesn’t cut it. Instead, it adds or removes chemical markers that can turn genes on or off. Another company, nChroma, has shown success in animals by lowering bad cholesterol and is now testing treatments for hepatitis B in humans.

**Impact** – This approach is important because it changes gene activity without altering the DNA code, making it safer and reversible. Unlike traditional gene editing that cuts DNA and risks mistakes, epigenetic editing avoids these dangers. It can treat diseases that were hard to tackle before, like certain muscular disorders, high cholesterol, and chronic viral infections. The technology also uses small proteins and harmless viruses to deliver treatments, making it easier to apply in patients.

**What's next step** – Clinical trials are underway to test these therapies in people, including those with hepatitis B and FSHD. Researchers will watch carefully for safety and effectiveness. If successful, this method could lead to new treatments for many diseases by precisely controlling gene activity without changing the DNA sequence.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists are developing safer gene therapies that treat diseases by changing chemical markers on DNA instead of cutting the DNA itself, offering new hope for conditions like muscular dystrophy, high cholesterol, and hepatitis B.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode d’édition génétique cible les maladies en modifiant les marqueurs de l’ADN sans le couper
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des scientifiques ont mis au point une nouvelle façon de traiter les maladies en modifiant les marqueurs chimiques sur l’ADN plutôt qu’en coupant ou réécrivant l’ADN lui-même. Cette méthode s’appelle l’édition épigénétique ciblée et utilise un outil lié à CRISPR, une technologie célèbre pour l’édition génétique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une start-up appelée Epicrispr Biotechnologies, dirigée par Amber Salzman depuis 2021, teste cette nouvelle thérapie pour traiter la dystrophie musculaire facioscapulohumérale (FSHD), une maladie musculaire rare. L’entreprise utilise une version modifiée de CRISPR appelée « Cas9 mort » ou Cas12F, qui se fixe à l’ADN sans le couper. Au lieu de cela, elle ajoute ou enlève des marqueurs chimiques qui peuvent activer ou désactiver des gènes. Une autre société, nChroma, a montré des résultats positifs chez les animaux en réduisant le mauvais cholestérol et teste maintenant des traitements contre l’hépatite B chez l’humain.

**Impact** – Cette approche est importante car elle modifie l’activité des gènes sans changer le code ADN, ce qui la rend plus sûre et réversible. Contrairement à l’édition génétique traditionnelle qui coupe l’ADN et peut causer des erreurs, l’édition épigénétique évite ces risques. Elle peut traiter des maladies difficiles à soigner auparavant, comme certaines dystrophies musculaires, le cholestérol élevé et les infections virales chroniques. La technologie utilise aussi de petites protéines et des virus inoffensifs pour délivrer les traitements, ce qui facilite leur utilisation chez les patients.

**Prochaine étape** – Des essais cliniques sont en cours pour tester ces thérapies chez l’homme, notamment pour l’hépatite B et la FSHD. Les chercheurs surveilleront attentivement la sécurité et l’efficacité. Si cela fonctionne, cette méthode pourrait permettre de nouveaux traitements pour de nombreuses maladies en contrôlant précisément l’activité des gènes sans modifier la séquence ADN.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques développent des thérapies géniques plus sûres qui traitent les maladies en modifiant les marqueurs chimiques sur l’ADN plutôt qu’en coupant l’ADN, offrant de nouvelles perspectives pour des maladies comme la dystrophie musculaire, le cholestérol élevé et l’hépatite B.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo enfoque de edición genética trata enfermedades modificando marcadores del ADN sin cortarlo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Científicos han desarrollado una nueva forma de tratar enfermedades cambiando los marcadores químicos en el ADN en lugar de cortar o reescribir el ADN. Este método se llama edición epigenética dirigida y usa una herramienta relacionada con CRISPR, tecnología conocida por la edición genética.

**Qué pasó** – Una empresa emergente llamada Epicrispr Biotechnologies, liderada por Amber Salzman desde 2021, está probando esta nueva terapia para tratar la distrofia muscular facioescapulohumeral (FSHD), una enfermedad muscular rara. La compañía usa una versión modificada de CRISPR llamada “Cas9 muerto” o Cas12F, que se une al ADN pero no lo corta. En vez de eso, añade o quita marcadores químicos que pueden encender o apagar genes. Otra empresa, nChroma, ha mostrado éxito en animales al reducir el colesterol malo y ahora está probando tratamientos para hepatitis B en humanos.

**Impacto** – Este método es importante porque cambia la actividad genética sin alterar el código ADN, lo que lo hace más seguro y reversible. A diferencia de la edición genética tradicional que corta el ADN y puede causar errores, la edición epigenética evita estos riesgos. Puede tratar enfermedades que antes eran difíciles, como ciertas distrofias musculares, colesterol alto e infecciones virales crónicas. La tecnología también usa proteínas pequeñas y virus inofensivos para administrar los tratamientos, facilitando su uso en pacientes.

**Próximo paso** – Se están realizando ensayos clínicos para probar estas terapias en personas, incluyendo hepatitis B y FSHD. Los investigadores vigilarán cuidadosamente la seguridad y eficacia. Si tienen éxito, este método podría crear nuevos tratamientos para muchas enfermedades controlando la actividad genética sin cambiar la secuencia del ADN.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos están desarrollando terapias genéticas más seguras que tratan enfermedades modificando marcadores químicos en el ADN sin cortar el ADN, ofreciendo nuevas esperanzas para condiciones como distrofia muscular, colesterol alto y hepatitis B.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing double helix DNA strand gently wrapped with delicate, colorful chemical markers like tiny glowing beads, surrounded by soft, flowing waves of light symbolizing precise editing; nearby, a stylized virus particle made of smooth, rounded shapes dissolves peacefully into the background, all rendered in natural, earthy tones and soft pastels.

articles.title: Ligand-enabled distal desaturative lactonization of aliphatic acids
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10826-8Ligand-enabled distal desaturative lactonization of aliphatic acids
articles.content: Transition metal catalysis serves as a fundamental strategy for transforming inert C−H bonds into valuable functional motifs.1,2 However, achieving regioselective activation of remote C−H bonds remains challenging, particularly in unbiased hydrocarbon frameworks.2,3 In this context, distal C(sp3)−H bonds are especially difficult to functionalize, as conformational flexibility favors proximal C−H activation.3,4 In this study, we demonstrate a ligand-enabled strategy using designed O-allyl amido ester (OAAE) ligands for palladium-catalyzed activation of γ-methylene and methine C−H sites in unbiased aliphatic carboxylic acids, derived from readily available fatty and cyclic acid feedstocks. This protocol enables direct transformation of aliphatic carboxylic acid substrates into distal desaturated γ-lactones and double dehydrogenated γ-spirolactones. Mechanistic studies are consistent with a pathway involving Pd(II)-mediated γ-C(sp3)−H activation, followed by dehydrogenation and intramolecular cyclization. These lactones with an unsaturated arm, serve as key intermediates for the formation of complex natural products and pharmaceuticals. For instance, muricatacin (from soursop/laxman phal) and its analogue were rapidly assembled in three steps from margaric acid using this strategy and evaluated for anticancer activity, thereby demonstrating the potential of our approach for providing a rapid access to biologically relevant frameworks for traditional medicine. The introduced distal desaturation further opens up new avenues for remote functionalization, streamlining access to diverse bioactive molecules with improved step and atom economy.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant chemical breakthrough enabling selective activation of remote C−H bonds in aliphatic acids, facilitating rapid synthesis of biologically relevant molecules including anticancer agents. This advancement has broad implications for pharmaceutical development and traditional medicine, offering a new efficient method to access complex bioactive compounds with potential health benefits.
articles.personality_title: New chemical method speeds up making important natural medicines
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Chemists often try to change parts of molecules called C−H bonds to create useful substances. But it’s hard to change the ones far away from main groups in flexible molecules, like fatty acids.

**What happened** – Scientists found a way to use special helper molecules called ligands with palladium metal to target and change these distant C−H bonds in aliphatic acids. This process creates special ring-shaped molecules called lactones, which are important in making natural medicines. They showed this method works by quickly making a compound from soursop fruit that can fight cancer.

**Impact** – This method lets scientists work on parts of molecules that were hard to reach before. It makes it faster and easier to build complex molecules found in medicines, saving time and materials. That could help develop new drugs and improve traditional remedies.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will likely explore using this approach to make other useful natural products and medicines. They may also try to improve the method to work with more types of molecules.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new chemical technique allows precise changes to difficult parts of fatty acids, speeding up the creation of important natural medicines like anticancer compounds.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode chimique accélère la fabrication de médicaments naturels importants
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les chimistes essaient souvent de modifier des parties des molécules appelées liaisons C−H pour créer des substances utiles. Mais il est difficile de modifier celles éloignées des groupes principaux dans des molécules flexibles, comme les acides gras.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont trouvé un moyen d’utiliser des molécules auxiliaires spéciales, appelées ligands, avec du palladium pour cibler et modifier ces liaisons C−H éloignées dans les acides aliphatique. Ce procédé crée des molécules en forme d’anneau appelées lactones, importantes pour fabriquer des médicaments naturels. Ils ont montré que cette méthode fonctionne en fabriquant rapidement un composé issu du fruit de corossol capable de combattre le cancer.

**Impact** – Cette méthode permet aux scientifiques de travailler sur des parties des molécules auparavant difficiles à atteindre. Elle facilite et accélère la construction de molécules complexes utilisées en médecine, économisant temps et matériaux. Cela pourrait aider à développer de nouveaux médicaments et améliorer les remèdes traditionnels.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement explorer l’utilisation de cette approche pour fabriquer d’autres produits naturels et médicaments utiles. Ils pourraient aussi essayer d’améliorer la méthode pour qu’elle fonctionne avec plus de types de molécules.

**Phrase clé** – Une nouvelle technique chimique permet de modifier précisément des parties difficiles des acides gras, accélérant la création de médicaments naturels importants comme des composés anticancéreux.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método químico acelera la fabricación de importantes medicinas naturales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los químicos suelen intentar cambiar partes de las moléculas llamadas enlaces C−H para crear sustancias útiles. Pero es difícil cambiar los que están lejos de los grupos principales en moléculas flexibles, como los ácidos grasos.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos encontraron una forma de usar moléculas especiales llamadas ligandos junto con paladio para apuntar y cambiar estos enlaces C−H distantes en ácidos alifáticos. Este proceso crea moléculas en forma de anillo llamadas lactonas, importantes para fabricar medicinas naturales. Mostraron que este método funciona al hacer rápidamente un compuesto del fruto de guanábana que puede combatir el cáncer.

**Impacto** – Este método permite a los científicos trabajar en partes de las moléculas que antes eran difíciles de alcanzar. Facilita y acelera la construcción de moléculas complejas usadas en medicinas, ahorrando tiempo y materiales. Esto podría ayudar a desarrollar nuevos medicamentos y mejorar remedios tradicionales.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente explorarán usar este método para hacer otros productos naturales y medicinas útiles. También podrían mejorar el método para que funcione con más tipos de moléculas.

**Frase clave** – Una nueva técnica química permite cambiar con precisión partes difíciles de los ácidos grasos, acelerando la creación de medicinas naturales importantes como compuestos anticancerígenos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed warm-toned painting of a stylized chemical flask gently glowing with soft golden light, surrounded by elegant, swirling ribbons resembling molecular ligands entwining a delicate, partially formed lactone ring made of smooth, flowing curves, set against a muted natural background of fatty acid-inspired organic shapes and subtle, abstract representations of soursop fruit and medicinal herbs.

articles.title: Researchers discover why fructose doesn't satisfy hunger like glucose
articles.summary: A new study found that fructose and glucose may look the same on a nutrition label, but the brain treats them very differently. In mice, glucose strongly reduced activity in hunger-promoting brain cells, while fructose had a much weaker effect. High-fructose corn syrup triggered a stronger response and was preferred by the animals. The findings suggest that the type of sugar—not just the calories—can influence appetite and food preferences.
articles.content:
Fructose and glucose are two common sugars found in many foods and drinks. Although they contain the same number of calories, new research suggests the brain responds to them in very different ways.

Scientists at the Monell Chemical Senses Center discovered that fructose and glucose communicate with the brain through separate gut-brain pathways. Their findings indicate that these differences may influence food and beverage preferences and could help explain why certain sweetened products are especially appealing.

The study, published June 10 in the journal Neuron, identified a specific signaling route that allows fructose to communicate with the brain. In experiments involving mice, researchers found that this pathway was far less effective than the one used by glucose when it came to reducing activity in neurons associated with hunger.

"This work adds to our growing understanding of how modern diets, especially those high in fructose or high-fructose corn syrup, interact with the neural systems involved in appetite," said senior author and Monell Member Amber Alhadeff, PhD.

How Fructose and Glucose Affect Hunger Neurons

To investigate how the sugars influence the brain, researchers recorded neural activity in mice after exposure to fructose and glucose.

The team found that fructose increased levels of the gut hormone PYY. That hormone then signaled through the vagus nerve, leading to a modest reduction in the activity of agouti-related protein (AgRP) neurons, which play a major role in driving hunger. When researchers disrupted this pathway, fructose could no longer affect those neurons.

Glucose produced a very different response. According to the researchers, it did not rely on the same PYY-Y2 vagus nerve pathway. Instead, glucose strongly suppressed AgRP neuron activity, resulting in a much larger effect on hunger-related brain signaling.

Sugar Type Influenced Food Preferences

Although fructose and glucose produced similar short-term effects on food intake, the mice eventually developed preferences that corresponded to the degree of AgRP neuron inhibition triggered by each sugar.

The researchers also examined high-fructose corn syrup (HFCS), a widely used sweetener made from a combination of fructose and glucose. The mice showed a preference for HFCS, and the sweetener suppressed AgRP neuron activity more strongly than fructose alone.

According to the researchers, this stronger effect on hunger-related neurons may help explain why foods and beverages containing HFCS can be particularly appealing.

Challenging Assumptions About Calories and Hunger

The results call into question a long-held assumption that AgRP neurons primarily track calorie intake regardless of where those calories come from.

Instead, the findings suggest that these hunger-related neurons can distinguish between different sugars and respond through separate biological pathways. Even though fructose and glucose provide the same amount of energy, the mice's brains processed them differently.

The study highlights the complexity of nutrient sensing in the body and suggests that even simple sugars can have distinct effects on the gut, the brain, and behavior.

This research was supported by grants R01DK131558, DP2AT011965, R01DK116004, F31DK13558, and S10OD030354 from the National Institutes of Health; the American Heart Association; the New York Stem Cell Foundation; the Klingenstein Fund; the Simons Foundation, the Pew Charitable Trusts, the Penn Institute for Diabetes, Obesity, and Metabolism; the Hearst Fellowship, and the Monell Chemical Senses Center.
articles.reasoning: The study reveals a significant scientific discovery about how different sugars affect hunger and brain signaling, which has broad implications for understanding diet, appetite control, and potentially obesity and metabolic health. It provides detailed, substantive findings with meaningful real-world health impact beyond a niche audience.
articles.personality_title: New study reveals why fructose doesn't curb hunger like glucose
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Fructose and glucose are sugars found in many foods and drinks. Even though they have the same calories, scientists wanted to understand why they seem to affect hunger differently.

**What happened** – Researchers at the Monell Chemical Senses Center studied how these sugars influence hunger in mice. They found that glucose strongly reduces activity in brain cells that make animals feel hungry, while fructose has a much weaker effect. They also tested high-fructose corn syrup (HFCS), which caused a stronger brain response and was preferred by the mice.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that the brain treats different sugars in unique ways, not just based on calories. It helps explain why foods with HFCS might be more appealing and why fructose doesn’t satisfy hunger as well. This could change how we think about sugar’s role in diet and appetite.

**What's next step** – Scientists hope to study these effects in humans and explore how this knowledge might help develop better ways to control appetite or address health issues like obesity.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The brain uses different pathways to respond to fructose and glucose, affecting hunger and food preferences beyond calorie content.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude révèle pourquoi le fructose ne coupe pas la faim comme le glucose
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le fructose et le glucose sont des sucres présents dans de nombreux aliments et boissons. Même s'ils contiennent les mêmes calories, les scientifiques voulaient comprendre pourquoi ils influencent différemment la sensation de faim.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs du Monell Chemical Senses Center ont étudié comment ces sucres agissent sur la faim chez la souris. Ils ont découvert que le glucose réduit fortement l'activité des cellules cérébrales qui provoquent la faim, tandis que le fructose a un effet beaucoup plus faible. Ils ont aussi testé le sirop de maïs à haute teneur en fructose (HFCS), qui a provoqué une réponse cérébrale plus forte et était préféré par les souris.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que le cerveau traite les différents sucres de manière unique, pas seulement en fonction des calories. Cela aide à comprendre pourquoi les aliments avec HFCS peuvent être plus attractifs et pourquoi le fructose ne calme pas la faim aussi bien. Cela pourrait changer notre façon de voir le rôle du sucre dans l'alimentation et l'appétit.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent étudier ces effets chez l'humain et voir comment cette connaissance pourrait aider à mieux contrôler l'appétit ou à traiter des problèmes de santé comme l'obésité.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le cerveau utilise des voies différentes pour répondre au fructose et au glucose, ce qui influence la faim et les préférences alimentaires au-delà des calories.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo estudio revela por qué la fructosa no satisface el hambre como la glucosa
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La fructosa y la glucosa son azúcares que se encuentran en muchos alimentos y bebidas. Aunque tienen las mismas calorías, los científicos querían entender por qué afectan el hambre de manera diferente.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Monell Chemical Senses Center estudiaron cómo estos azúcares influyen en el hambre en ratones. Descubrieron que la glucosa reduce fuertemente la actividad de las células cerebrales que generan hambre, mientras que la fructosa tiene un efecto mucho más débil. También probaron el jarabe de maíz alto en fructosa (HFCS), que provocó una respuesta cerebral más fuerte y fue preferido por los ratones.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que el cerebro trata los diferentes azúcares de forma única, no solo basándose en las calorías. Ayuda a explicar por qué los alimentos con HFCS pueden ser más atractivos y por qué la fructosa no satisface el hambre igual. Esto podría cambiar cómo pensamos sobre el papel del azúcar en la dieta y el apetito.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan estudiar estos efectos en humanos y explorar cómo este conocimiento podría ayudar a controlar mejor el apetito o tratar problemas de salud como la obesidad.

**Resumen en una frase** – El cerebro usa vías diferentes para responder a la fructosa y la glucosa, afectando el hambre y las preferencias alimentarias más allá del contenido calórico.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two distinct glowing pathways—one golden and vibrant representing glucose, strongly illuminating a calm, resting brain silhouette with subtle neuron patterns, and the other a softer, dimmer blue pathway symbolizing fructose, gently connecting a stylized gut shaped like a fruit to the brain via a delicate nerve-like bridge, set against a simple natural background with muted earth tones.

articles.title: Oo oo, ha ha: why humans and great apes giggle alike when tickled
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 25 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01994-8The rhythmic patterns of laughter found in apes and humans reveal that complex primate vocal control might have started evolving 15 million years ago.
articles.content:
When laughing, chimpanzees and other great apes produce similar patterns of vocalization to human children.Credit: Jean-Philippe Ksiazek/AFP via Getty

Tickling a chimpanzee, a gorilla and a human child causes similar rhythms of laughter, according to an analysis of primate behaviour.

Laughter is a conserved vocalization in humans and their closest primate relatives. A study published in Communications Biology on 25 June1 found that their laughter followed consistent rhythmic patterns. When great apes and humans between six months and seven years old were tickled, they left evenly spaced intervals between successive laughing sounds.

The authors suggest that these regular rhythms reflect how nuanced primate vocal motor control can be and that it might have been inherited from a common ancestor that lived 15 million years ago.

The analysis “falls in line with an emerging body of data now that suggest that our closest living relatives, the primates, and particularly the great apes, have more control over their vocal system than previously thought”, says Simon Townsend, who studies primate communication at the University of Zurich in Switzerland.

The findings also provide insights for the “evolutionary progression of vocal flexibility within the primate lineage”, he adds.

Apes and japes

Study co-author Chiara De Gregorio, a primatologist at the University of Warwick in Coventry, UK, and her colleagues compared recordings of juvenile and infant great apes — including four orangutans (Pongo pygmaeus), two gorillas (Gorilla gorilla), three bonobos (Pan paniscus) and four chimpanzees (Pan troglodytes) — with recordings of four human children playing with their mothers at home.

The analysis focused on 140 sequences of laughter, of which 42 were from bonobos, 35 from chimpanzees, 34 from gorillas, 16 from orangutans and 13 from the human children.

De Gregorio and her colleagues examined how much time passed between successive bursts of laughter and found that both humans and apes stuck to a consistent rhythm when tickled.

However, laughter during social play had more variability. “While in play, laughter was a bit messy because, if we think of two animals playing or two kids playing, so many things happen,” explains De Gregorio. “They may roll or they may play fight.” Physical movements could affect breathing patterns in ways that make it difficult to keep the rhythm of laughter consistent.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a scientific discovery about the evolutionary origins of laughter and vocal control in humans and great apes, providing meaningful insights into primate communication and human evolution. This knowledge has broad significance for understanding human biology and social behavior, which can inspire curiosity and hope about our shared heritage and cognitive capacities.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find humans and great apes laugh in the same rhythm when tickled
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Laughter is a common sound among humans and their closest relatives, great apes. Scientists have been curious about how similar ape laughter is to human laughter and what it might tell us about evolution.

**What happened** – Researchers studied recordings of laughter from young great apes, including chimpanzees, gorillas, bonobos, and orangutans, as well as human children aged six months to seven years. They found that when tickled, both humans and apes laughed with a steady rhythm, leaving even gaps between each laugh sound.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that great apes have more control over their voices than we thought. The shared rhythm suggests that this ability to laugh in a controlled way may have come from a common ancestor that lived about 15 million years ago. This helps us understand how vocal sounds like laughter evolved in primates.

**What's next step** – Scientists will likely explore more about how primates control their voices in other situations and how this ability developed over time. This could reveal more about how human speech and emotions evolved.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Humans and great apes share a rhythmic pattern in laughter, pointing to a vocal skill that began evolving millions of years ago.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les humains et les grands singes rient au même rythme lorsqu’ils sont chatouillés, selon les scientifiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le rire est un son courant chez les humains et leurs plus proches parents, les grands singes. Les scientifiques se demandent depuis longtemps à quel point le rire des singes ressemble à celui des humains et ce que cela révèle sur l’évolution.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont étudié des enregistrements de rires de jeunes grands singes, comme les chimpanzés, gorilles, bonobos et orangs-outans, ainsi que des enfants humains âgés de six mois à sept ans. Ils ont découvert que lorsqu’on les chatouille, humains et singes rient avec un rythme régulier, laissant des pauses égales entre chaque son de rire.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les grands singes contrôlent leur voix mieux qu’on ne le pensait. Ce rythme partagé suggère que cette capacité à rire de manière contrôlée vient d’un ancêtre commun qui a vécu il y a environ 15 millions d’années. Cela nous aide à comprendre comment les sons vocaux comme le rire ont évolué chez les primates.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont probablement étudier comment les primates contrôlent leur voix dans d’autres situations et comment cette capacité s’est développée au fil du temps. Cela pourrait révéler comment la parole et les émotions humaines ont évolué.

**Phrase à retenir** – Les humains et les grands singes partagent un rythme dans leur rire, ce qui montre une capacité vocale qui a commencé à évoluer il y a des millions d’années.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren que humanos y grandes simios ríen con el mismo ritmo al ser cosquilleados
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La risa es un sonido común entre los humanos y sus parientes más cercanos, los grandes simios. Los científicos han querido saber qué tan parecido es el risa de los simios al de los humanos y qué puede decirnos sobre la evolución.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores estudiaron grabaciones de risa de jóvenes grandes simios, como chimpancés, gorilas, bonobos y orangutanes, así como de niños humanos de seis meses a siete años. Encontraron que cuando los cosquillean, tanto humanos como simios ríen con un ritmo constante, dejando pausas iguales entre cada sonido de risa.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra que los grandes simios tienen más control sobre sus voces de lo que se pensaba. El ritmo compartido sugiere que esta habilidad para reír de forma controlada viene de un ancestro común que vivió hace unos 15 millones de años. Esto ayuda a entender cómo evolucionaron los sonidos vocales como la risa en los primates.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos probablemente explorarán más cómo los primates controlan su voz en otras situaciones y cómo esta habilidad se desarrolló con el tiempo. Esto podría revelar más sobre cómo evolucionaron el habla y las emociones humanas.

**Una frase para recordar** – Humanos y grandes simios comparten un patrón rítmico en la risa, señalando una habilidad vocal que comenzó a evolucionar hace millones de años.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of joyful silhouettes of a young child and four great apes—a chimpanzee, gorilla, bonobo, and orangutan—seated in a circle, their mouths open in rhythmic laughter, surrounded by soft, flowing sound waves rhythmically connecting them, all rendered in gentle, natural earth tones.
